name: Xavier Cugat
abstract: Xavier Cugat (; Catalan: [ʃəβiˈe kuˈɣat]; 1 January 1900 – 27 October 1990) was an American musician and bandleader who was a leading figure in the spread of Latin music in the United States. Originally from Girona, Catalonia in Spain, he spent his formative years in Havana, Cuba, before arriving in New York City in 1915.

concerts.label: Grand Concert featuring Hipolito Lazaro and Others

works.t: Sadko: Song of the Indian guest

about_quotes.speaker: Victor Rendon
about_quotes.evidence: then you have the rumbas of Xavier Cugat, you know and all that, from back in the 30s, 40s and 50s.
about_quotes.doc_title: Victor Rendon interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, November 15, 2019 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Frank "Machito" Grillo
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Xavier Cugat in the context of a business negotiation ('I did a trick') and historical precedence regarding the conga drum ('Before we did, Xavier Cugat'), but does not describe a personal relationship, collaboration, or direct interaction beyond this professional/historical

about_quotes.speaker: Horace Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: Xavier Cugat, Robert Walker, and Lena Horne on the same bill... All I had Xavier Cugat's band do during our four weeks stay was on the end, when we finished playing, was to hit the chord.

about_quotes.speaker: Red Callender
about_quotes.evidence: Xavier Cugat's band was the featured orchestra, and Lena Horne was the featured attraction. And she took Arnold Ross and Irving Ashby and myself. We were her trio.

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: And Xavier Cugat's band was on the stage in one of the theatres and all his instruments got wet... I really didn't get to audition again until June of that year.

about_quotes.speaker: Sol Yaged
about_quotes.evidence: Xavier Cugat played, and there was no Roosevelt Hotel anymore where they had music with Guy Lombardo.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sol Yaged interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000
